impact:
A field report on building 52 production agents surfaces concrete, transferable prompt-design patterns our prompt-engineering skills should teach/enforce. Each maps to a way vague prompt guidance underperforms at scale (a single bad response in a one-off prompt becomes a recurring pattern when an agent is reused by many people):

1. Explicit "WHAT YOU DO NOT DO" lists beat aspirational accuracy instructions. Named prohibitions ("do not fabricate pain points/priorities not in the input"; "do not open with 'I hope this email finds you well' or any filler warm-up") stop the model filling gaps with plausible content. Generic "be accurate" does not.

2. Source-quality labels beat "cite your sources". A mandatory output structure (e.g. tagging each item [News] / [PR] / [Analysis] and flagging when [PR] dominates) is a constraint the structure enforces; "cite your sources" is just a hope.

3. Named pattern lists beat generic checks. Instead of "avoid biased language", enumerate the patterns and the reason for each ("rockstar/ninja/guru" = masculine-coded; "native English speaker" = national-origin risk; "cultural fit" without defined criteria = can mask bias). The named version catches what the generic one misses and explains the why.

4. Every agent should end with a QUALITY SELF-CHECK it runs against its own output before delivering. Without one, the model decides what "good enough" means and it is always "good enough".

5. Conversation starters / input prompts must be specific ("Paste an objection and I'll classify it, find the real concern, and build a structured response"), not vague ("Help me with my email"). Specific input -> specific output.

6. One agent, one job. Overloaded instruction blocks that handle 5 document types do none well. Narrow scope on purpose; also respect platform char limits (the source capped each block at 8,000 chars for Copilot Studio).

Action: audit prompt-engineering-author (does it prompt authors to add negative/prohibition lists, output-structure constraints, named-pattern checks, a self-check section, and to narrow scope?) and prompt-engineering-improve (does its review rubric flag missing prohibitions, vague checks, and overloaded scope?). Fold the strongest patterns into the skill guidance/checklists.

Source: Reddit field report on awesome-copilot-chat-agents (https://github.com/kesslernity/awesome-copilot-chat-agents) — same design principles as the NerdyChefs prompts (specific input, explicit guardrails, named failure modes), deployed as 52 agents across 10 domains.
resolution: Folded patterns into prompt-engineering-author (Rung 3 Guardrails + anti-pattern reconciliation) and prompt-engineering-improve (fabrication/filler diagnosis); durable spec updated. T-030.
title: Adopt agent-prompt design patterns (negative lists, source-quality labels, named-pattern checks, quality self-check, narrow scope)
